title_en: Fasting: Rules, Exemptions, and Make-up Days
content_en:
The chapter on fasting in Al-Mughni reflects the Hanbali school's characteristic precision in defining exactly what breaks the fast, who is excused from it, and what obligations arise from violations. Ibn Qudamah draws extensively on hadith evidence and engages with the positions of the other schools in detail.

The Hanbali school holds that Ramadan fasting becomes obligatory upon sighting the crescent moon, or by completing thirty days of Sha'ban if the moon is not sighted due to clouds or haze. The Prophet said: "Fast when you see it (the moon) and break the fast when you see it; if it is obscured from you, complete thirty days of Sha'ban" (Bukhari, Muslim). Ibn Qudamah notes the scholarly debate over whether the moon sighting of one locality is binding on all Muslims — the majority position (including Hanbali) being that sightings are locality-specific, not universal.

The intention (niyyah) for Ramadan fasting must be made each night before Fajr, according to the Hanbali school. This is based on the hadith: "Whoever does not make the intention to fast before dawn has no fast" (Abu Dawud). The Hanbali school is strict here, rejecting the Hanafi position that a single intention at the start of Ramadan covers the whole month for obligatory fasts.

The Hanbali school identifies the acts that break the fast (muftirat) as: eating or drinking anything intentionally; deliberate vomiting; sexual intercourse; anything that carries nutritive value reaching the stomach through an unconventional route (such as nasal drops or intravenous feeding); and the beginning of menstruation or post-natal bleeding. The school distinguishes between forgetful eating (which does not break the fast, based on the hadith of the Prophet: "Whoever forgets and eats or drinks, let him complete his fast — Allah has fed him and given him drink," Bukhari) and deliberate eating, which requires making up the day.

The kaffarah (expiation) for deliberately breaking the fast through sexual intercourse is one of the most serious obligations in Islamic law: freeing a slave, or fasting sixty consecutive days, or feeding sixty poor people — in that order. Ibn Qudamah discusses whether the kaffarah applies to other deliberate violations and concludes that it is specific to sexual intercourse, with only qada' (make-up) being required for other deliberate violations.

Exemptions from fasting are carefully detailed: the sick may break the fast and make up the days; travelers above a minimum journey distance may break the fast (with the Hanbali school setting three marhalah — approximately 80 km — as the minimum qualifying journey); pregnant and nursing women who fear harm may break the fast and make up days, and some scholars add fidyah (feeding a poor person for each day); the elderly permanently incapable of fasting pay fidyah in lieu of make-up days.

Ibn Qudamah also discusses the virtues and rules of voluntary fasting: the six days of Shawwal, fasting Mondays and Thursdays, Arafah day, Ashura (9th and 10th of Muharram), and the three middle days of each month (the ayam al-bid). He notes the prohibition on singling out Friday or Saturday for fasting without combining it with the adjacent day, and the prohibition of fasting on the two Eids and the days of Tashriq (11th–13th Dhul-Hijjah).

The comprehensive nature of this chapter demonstrates the Hanbali school's commitment to deriving every ruling from its evidential foundation in Quran and Sunnah.
title_id: Puasa: Ketentuan, Keringanan, dan Hari-hari Qadha
content_id:
Pembahasan puasa dalam Al-Mughni mencerminkan pendekatan Ibn Qudamah yang menyeluruh terhadap ibadah. Beliau memulai dengan menetapkan dasar wajibnya puasa Ramadan dari Al-Quran dan Sunnah, kemudian membahas secara terperinci semua aspek praktis dan hukum yang berkaitan dengan ibadah ini.

Topik-topik yang dibahas meliputi: penetapan awal Ramadan (rukyah dan hisab), niat puasa, hal-hal yang membatalkan puasa (makan, minum, hubungan suami-istri, muntah dengan sengaja, dll.), kafarat bagi yang membatalkan puasa dengan sengaja, golongan yang mendapat keringanan (musafir, sakit, hamil, menyusui, lansia), qadha puasa Ramadan, puasa sunnah dan hari-hari yang dilarang berpuasa, serta itikaf di bulan Ramadan. Ibn Qudamah membahas setiap masalah dengan kedalaman ilmiah yang mengesankan.
